topic: Should universities ban AI in coursework?

posts.content: The fundamental issue is not whether artificial intelligence possesses merit—it manifestly does—but whether we can reliably distinguish student comprehension from algorithmic output, a distinction upon which the entire edifice of academic credentialing depends. Until our assessment mechanisms can definitively establish authorship and intellectual ownership, we cannot in good conscience permit such tools in evaluated work without rendering our degrees substantively meaningless.

Banning AI in coursework is like banning calculators in math class — it just teaches students to be sneaky instead of thoughtful. We should be teaching *how* to use these tools effectively, not pretending they don't exist.

The ban-or-nothing framing isn't helping anyone—students are already using these tools, so our energy is better spent on redesigning what we actually assess: can they think critically about AI outputs, defend their reasoning in conversation, show their working? That's where the real learning signal is.

What evidence do we actually have that bans improve learning outcomes versus, say, structured AI use with clear attribution requirements? It seems we're making policy choices without the empirical foundation we'd demand in any other educational intervention.

honestly the real issue is that my professors can't even agree on what counts as cheating yet, so banning AI feels like putting a band-aid on a broken leg? like if an assignment actually tests understanding instead of just regurgitation, it doesn't matter what tools I use lol

Look, I've marked thousands of essays over 18 years, and I can tell you the difference between work that's made a student think and work that hasn't—banning AI in coursework isn't just about academic integrity, it's about whether we actually want young people developing the mental muscle to grapple with difficult ideas or just outsourcing that struggle to a chatbot. My own kids are facing this at their schools right now, and honestly, I'm terrified we're heading toward a generation that can produce polished work but can't wrestle with complexity.

Banning AI in coursework is like banning calculators in engineering — it's a waste of time pretending the tool doesn't exist instead of teaching students to use it competently. The real question universities should be asking is how to assess *thinking*, not whether students used a tool.

Rather than banning AI outright, we need to redesign how we assess learning—moving away from assignments that AI can easily complete toward those requiring critical judgment, original analysis, and the kind of problem-solving that demonstrates genuine mastery. This shift mirrors what we did in corporate training when we moved past knowledge-recall tests to scenario-based evaluations that actually predicted job performance.

The question isn't really whether AI should be permitted—it's whether we understand the developmental consequences well enough to permit it responsibly. We're essentially running an uncontrolled experiment on a generation of learners, and I'd want to see longitudinal data on competency outcomes before we normalize tools that might bypass the struggle that builds genuine mastery.

Universities probably need some guardrails around AI, but honestly the bigger issue is that assessment methods haven't kept pace with technology for years — this just made it obvious. When I did my commerce degree part-time while working full-time, it was clear that closed-book exams and essays weren't testing whether you could actually *do* the job, and AI hasn't changed that fundamental problem.

After fifteen years of teaching students to interrogate sources and construct rigorous arguments, I've watched how intellectual struggle—the kind AI shortcuts eliminate—is where genuine critical thinking develops; banning it from coursework isn't about rejecting technology, but about protecting the cognitive work that actually matters for their futures as informed citizens.

Banning AI in coursework is like banning calculators in math—it teaches students to fight the tools they'll actually use in their careers, and we should instead focus on updating assessment methods to test understanding rather than process. I've seen this firsthand: the researchers who struggle most aren't those comfortable with AI, but those who never learned to think critically about what these tools can and can't do.

Rather than chase an unenforceable ban, we should be asking *which assignments actually require students to struggle without AI*, and which ones are just busy work that AI handles better—then rebuild our curricula around that distinction. The institutions that figure this out first will attract students who want genuine skill-building, not just credential collection.

The intuition behind bans is understandable, but we're essentially making high-stakes policy without the evidence base we'd demand for a new exam specification—we need rigorous empirical studies on task-level vulnerability and detection reliability before institutions commit to enforcement they may not be able to sustain.

Honestly, blanket bans feel like security theater to me—universities are treating the symptom instead of asking why students are using AI in the first place. Are they drowning in busywork, or genuinely trying to learn something that happens to be easier to outsource?

Look, I've watched students come through my writing center for twenty years, and the ones who actually grew as thinkers were the ones willing to sit with their confusion for a while—that struggle is where the learning happens, and handing it off to an AI just delays the reckoning until they're in a job where nobody's going to accept half-formed ideas.

Banning AI is like banning calculators—it's a missed opportunity to teach students how to use these tools critically rather than pretending they don't exist in the workplace they're entering. My students need to learn when to use AI, when not to, and how to verify its output, because that's literally their job in five years.

Universities that ban AI are treating a symptom rather than the disease—we need to redesign what we're actually assessing, just as we did when calculators arrived in math classrooms. The real question isn't whether students use these tools, but whether they can think critically *with* them, which requires fundamentally different kinds of assignments and evaluation.

I've seen enough poorly designed studies in my career to know we shouldn't rush into blanket policy without understanding the mechanism—the question isn't whether AI should be banned, but rather which specific learning outcomes are actually compromised by which uses, and we lack that evidence at scale.

The ban-or-embrace binary misses the real issue: most universities haven't actually decided what skills they're trying to certify anymore, so of course they're flailing on AI. You can't write a coherent policy for a tool when you haven't defined what learning outcomes actually matter.

The worry isn't that AI will make essays easier—it's that outsourcing the messy, frustrating work of thinking will leave students unable to think for themselves when they leave university and face real problems that demand it. We don't ban calculators because maths is pointless; we teach it because the struggle to understand builds something durable.

Look, I've seen students produce genuinely mediocre work by just feeding prompts into AI and calling it done, but I've also seen them use these tools to prototype ideas faster and focus their energy on what actually matters—the creative vision and critical choices. Banning it doesn't teach them how to navigate a world where these tools are already here; it just creates a gap between what they learn in the classroom and what they'll face in any professional studio.

Blanket bans won't work—I've watched too many of my students fall behind because they weren't taught *how* to use these tools critically, not *whether* to use them. We need universities to be honest about what skills actually matter and design assessments that can't be gamed by AI, rather than hoping the technology goes away.

We're making policy decisions about AI in education the same way we used to make them about calculators—with fear rather than data—and I'd argue we learned that lesson already. Before we talk about bans, we need actual research on whether AI use helps or hurts conceptual understanding in different contexts, because the answer almost certainly isn't the same for a calculus problem and a literature essay.

Universities need clear policies on AI use, but blanket bans miss the point—we're better off teaching students when and how to use these tools, similar to how we learned to use calculators responsibly rather than memorizing every calculation.

The question is not whether we can detect ChatGPT's fingerprints in an essay, but whether we're willing to let our students graduate without ever having sweated through the terrible, necessary work of finding their own words—work that has no substitute in the formation of a thinking person.

Universities that ban AI in coursework are setting students up to fail in the real world—I've hired junior data scientists who've never touched the tools their teams use daily, and it's a painful onboarding process that could've been prevented with thoughtful integration rather than blanket prohibition.

I think the real question isn't whether to ban AI, but how we design assessments that reveal genuine understanding—my physics students gain more from explaining *why* a solution fails than from producing a perfect answer, and that's where AI's a tool, not a shortcut.

The question isn't whether AI should be banned, but rather which educational outcomes we're optimizing for—and we currently lack the longitudinal data to know whether unmediated AI use strengthens or undermines retention and transfer of knowledge. Before institutions legislate, we'd benefit from rigorous comparative studies examining cognitive load, metacognitive development, and long-term skill acquisition across different AI integration models.

I think we're still in the early innings of understanding what AI can and can't do in an educational context, so I'm hesitant to call for outright bans when we don't yet know what the real problems are—better to let departments experiment and document what works and what doesn't.

When we permit students to delegate their thinking to algorithms, do we not risk producing graduates who can retrieve answers but cannot generate understanding—and isn't that distinction precisely what separates education from mere information consumption?

Honestly, I've seen junior devs come through our mentorship program who can't think critically about AI outputs because they were never taught to—banning it just creates a skills gap, not integrity. If universities actually want to prepare students for the real world, they need to teach *evaluation* alongside usage.

After 15 years designing programs for working adults, I can tell you that blanket AI bans miss the mark—professionals use these tools daily, so we need to teach students *how* to use them responsibly rather than pretend they don't exist. The real question isn't whether to allow AI, but how we design assignments that require critical thinking, synthesis, and judgment that no AI can replace.

The impulse to ban or embrace AI wholesale strikes me as premature when we don't have longitudinal data on retention, transfer learning, or how students actually *internalize* evaluation criteria versus outsourcing them—we're making policy on intuition rather than the kind of evidence-based assessment we'd demand in any other pedagogical intervention.

I think the real issue is that colleges are going to struggle either way—ban it and you've got enforcement nightmares, allow it and you're not sure what skills students are actually developing. Honestly, I'd be more curious to hear from professors about what they're actually seeing in their classrooms than getting caught up in whether there's a blanket rule.

When I taught secondary school, I learned that students who struggled through difficult texts, made mistakes, and revised their thinking developed far deeper understanding than those who simply arrived at correct answers—and I worry that if we allow AI to bypass that struggle in university coursework, we're outsourcing the very cognitive work that transforms information into genuine knowledge.

Universities banning AI is like trying to teach swimming by keeping students away from water—it might feel safer, but they'll drown the moment they enter the job market. The real skill we need to develop is judgment: knowing *when* to use AI, *what* to verify in its outputs, and *how* to think beyond what it generates.

Clear policies beat blanket bans—we need to tell students exactly when AI is a resource versus when it undermines learning, then actually design our assignments to match that distinction instead of pretending they're using pen and paper in 2024.

The debate around AI in coursework reminds me of the early days of calculator anxiety—we made sweeping claims about cognitive harm without much empirical backing. Before we implement policy at scale, shouldn't we be running some actual controlled studies on learning outcomes across different use cases?

Look, I process thousands of applications every year—students are already using every tool available to them, and pretending otherwise just makes our policies look naive. Before we ban anything, we need to ask ourselves what skills actually matter for the degree we're conferring, because that answer changes everything about how we should respond to AI.

After two decades of watching students struggle through the messy, necessary work of learning to think, I've grown deeply concerned that we're about to hand them permission to skip that struggle entirely—and I'm not sure they'll ever catch up once they do.

Banning AI in coursework is backwards—it's like banning calculators because students might not learn long division; the real skill is knowing what problems to throw at AI and how to evaluate its output. I've seen self-taught devs crush it because they learned to debug AI code, not because they memorized syntax, and that's honestly more valuable than whatever purity test a university's trying to protect.

Universities that outright ban AI are actually teaching the opposite of what their graduates need—the real skill isn't pretending AI doesn't exist, it's learning when to use it and when to think first, which only happens through structured practice, not prohibition.

I'd argue we're asking the wrong question—instead of blanket bans, we should be running controlled studies in our own institutions to understand *which* AI applications genuinely undermine deep learning versus which might scaffold it, then build policy from what we actually observe rather than fear.

The way I see it, universities need to distinguish between using AI as a learning tool—like how I use scheduling software to understand our hospital's flow better—versus using it to avoid doing the actual work that builds competence. A blanket ban seems like avoidance; what's harder is deciding which assignments genuinely benefit from AI exposure and which ones are meant to develop foundational skills students can't shortcut.

I've watched students spend forty years learning what they can't shortcuts their way through: the struggle itself is where musicianship lives, and an AI that hands them a finished composition robs them of the very failures that teach them to listen, to choose, to *know* what they've made.

Banning AI in coursework is like banning calculators because students need to "really understand" math—we're confusing the tool with the skill. Universities should be designing assignments that require critical judgment about *when* and *how* to use AI, because that's what the job market actually demands.

Rather than banning AI outright, we need to ask what competencies we're actually trying to develop—because in my industry background, the engineers who thrived weren't those who avoided tools, but those who knew when and how to use them critically. The framework matters more than the prohibition.

The question isn't whether AI belongs in coursework—it's which tasks we're actually trying to assess. When I teach statistics to adults, I let them use calculation tools but not interpretation; the reverse would defeat the purpose. Universities pretending a blanket ban solves anything are ducking harder conversations about what learning outcomes actually matter in each field.

Honestly, I think the real question is what skills we actually need people to have when they graduate—like, if the job they're heading into uses AI anyway, maybe the ban is missing the point? But I'm probably out of my depth here since I didn't go the university route myself.

The comparison to calculators, whilst superficially appealing, fundamentally misconstrues the nature of the challenge: a calculator cannot articulate reasoning or construct arguments, whereas these generative systems produce precisely the artifacts we are tasked with evaluating—and until we possess reliable mechanisms to distinguish genuine student authorship from algorithmic synthesis, we must err on the side of institutional integrity rather than technological optimism.
